exercises.hint: For each friend of the user, iterate their friends. Use a counter map to tally how many times each candidate appears, then sort by count.

problems.problem: Graph traversal queries timeout for celebrity accounts with millions of followers
problems.cause: High-degree nodes cause BFS to explode, touching millions of adjacency lists in a single hop
problems.solution: Cap the traversal fan-out by sampling edges for high-degree nodes, and serve precomputed results for known celebrity accounts.

problems.problem: Cross-shard graph queries have high latency due to network round trips
problems.cause: Friends are randomly distributed across shards, so a 2-hop query may hit dozens of shards
problems.solution: Use graph-aware partitioning (e.g., METIS) to co-locate densely connected subgraphs on the same shard, reducing cross-shard hops.

further_reading.title: TAO: Facebook's Distributed Data Store for the Social Graph
System Design: Social Graph at LinkedIn Scale

10.5.2.body:
## Design a Ride-Sharing Service (Uber)

### Requirements
- Match riders with nearby drivers in real time (under 5 seconds)
- Track live driver locations with updates every 3-5 seconds
- Handle surge pricing based on supply/demand ratios per zone
- Support trip lifecycle: request, match, pickup, in-progress, complete, payment

### Architecture
Drivers continuously report GPS locations to a Location Service that indexes them in a geospatial data structure. When a rider requests a trip, the Matching Service queries nearby available drivers, ranks them, and dispatches. A Trip Service manages the state machine for each ride. A separate Pricing Service computes dynamic fares.

### Key Components
- **Location Service**: Geohash or QuadTree index of active driver positions in Redis/memory
- **Matching Service**: Finds k-nearest available drivers, scores by ETA, rating, direction
- **Trip Service**: State machine (requested -> matched -> en_route -> in_trip -> completed)
- **Pricing Service**: Computes base fare + surge multiplier based on zone demand/supply ratio

### Scaling
Partition geographically by city or geohash prefix. Location updates are write-heavy; use in-memory stores. Matching is compute-heavy; scale independently. Use cell-based architecture for fault isolation per city.

### Trade-offs
- Geohash is simpler but creates edge-of-cell boundary issues; QuadTree adapts to density but is harder to distribute
- Greedy matching (nearest driver) is fast but globally suboptimal; batch matching optimizes overall wait time but adds latency
- Frequent location updates improve accuracy but increase bandwidth and processing cost
10.5.2.intuition: A ride-sharing system is a real-time geospatial matching problem where the core challenge is maintaining a constantly-updating spatial index of drivers and querying it fast enough to match riders before drivers move significantly. The cell-based architecture ensures that a failure in one city never cascades to another.

exercises.title: Build a Geohash-Based Nearest Driver Finder
exercises.description: Index 10,000 simulated driver locations using geohashes. Given a rider location, find the 5 nearest available drivers by expanding geohash rings. Measure query latency.
exercises.hint: Start with the rider's geohash cell, check for drivers. If fewer than 5 found, expand to the 8 neighboring cells. Repeat until enough candidates, then sort by actual distance.

exercises.title: Implement a Simple Surge Pricing Model
exercises.description: Divide a city into a grid of zones. Track rider requests and available drivers per zone. Compute a surge multiplier when demand/supply ratio exceeds 1.5x.
exercises.hint: Use a sliding window counter for requests and a point-in-time count for available drivers per zone. Multiplier = max(1.0, demand/supply * scaling_factor).

problems.problem: Riders at geohash cell boundaries get matched with far-away drivers while nearby ones in the adjacent cell are ignored
problems.cause: The search only checks the rider's own geohash cell and misses drivers just across the border
problems.solution: Always search the target cell plus all adjacent cells (9-cell neighborhood), then filter by actual Haversine distance.

problems.problem: Driver location updates are lost during peak hours causing stale matches
problems.cause: The location service write throughput is saturated and starts dropping UDP packets
problems.solution: Use a partitioned in-memory store (e.g., Redis Cluster) for location writes, and switch to TCP with backpressure to avoid silent data loss.

further_reading.title: Uber's Real-Time Market Platform
System Design Interview: Uber/Lyft Ride Sharing

10.5.3.body:
## Design an Online Code Editor (Replit)

### Requirements
- Provide a browser-based IDE with syntax highlighting, autocomplete, and file management
- Execute user code in isolated sandboxed environments per session
- Support multiple programming languages with language-specific toolchains
- Allow users to share and fork projects; persist workspace state

### Architecture
A web frontend delivers the editor UI (Monaco/CodeMirror). Each user session spins up an isolated container (Docker/microVM) with the appropriate language runtime. A WebSocket connection streams terminal I/O between browser and container. A workspace service persists files to object storage.

### Key Components
- **Editor Frontend**: Monaco editor in browser with LSP integration for autocomplete
- **Container Orchestrator**: Provisions, monitors, and recycles per-user sandboxes (Kubernetes/Firecracker)
- **Execution Engine**: Runs code inside the container, captures stdout/stderr, enforces resource limits
- **Workspace Storage**: Git-backed or object-store-backed file persistence per project

### Scaling
Pre-warm container pools per language to reduce cold start. Use Firecracker microVMs for fast boot (<150ms) and strong isolation. Auto-scale container hosts based on active session count.

### Trade-offs
- Containers are flexible but heavier; microVMs are lighter and more secure but less compatible
- Full LSP per session provides rich IDE features but is resource-intensive
- Persistent storage per project increases cost; ephemeral workspaces are cheaper but frustrate users
10.5.3.intuition: An online code editor is fundamentally a thin browser client connected to a per-user sandboxed compute environment, where the main challenge is provisioning and isolating thousands of concurrent sandboxes with low startup latency. Pre-warming container pools is the critical optimization that makes the experience feel instant.

exercises.title: Build a WebSocket-Based Terminal Relay
exercises.description: Create a server that spawns a subprocess (e.g., Python REPL) per WebSocket connection, relays stdin/stdout between the browser and the process, and cleans up on disconnect.
exercises.hint: Use pty.openpty() to create a pseudo-terminal, spawn the process attached to it, and use select/poll to shuttle bytes between the pty and the WebSocket.

exercises.title: Implement Resource-Limited Code Execution
exercises.description: Build an execution engine that runs user-submitted code with a 5-second timeout, 256MB memory limit, and no network access. Test with CPU bombs and fork bombs.
exercises.hint: Use cgroups (Linux) or ulimit to enforce memory and CPU limits. Use seccomp or a network namespace with no interfaces to block network access.

problems.problem: Cold start takes 10+ seconds when a user selects an uncommon language
problems.cause: No pre-warmed containers exist for that language, so the system must pull the image and start fresh
problems.solution: Maintain a small pre-warmed pool for all supported languages, and use layered container images with a shared base to speed up image pulls.

problems.problem: A malicious user escapes the container and accesses host resources
problems.cause: The container runs with excessive privileges or shares the host network namespace
problems.solution: Use rootless containers or Firecracker microVMs, apply seccomp profiles, drop all capabilities, and run with a read-only root filesystem.

further_reading.title: How Replit Went From a REPL to a Full IDE
Firecracker: Lightweight Virtualization for Serverless Applications

10.5.4.body:
## Design a Real-Time Collaborative Editor (Google Docs)

### Requirements
- Multiple users edit the same document simultaneously with <100ms perceived latency
- All users converge to the same document state regardless of edit order
- Support rich text formatting, comments, cursor presence, and revision history
- Handle offline edits that sync when connectivity is restored

### Architecture
Clients apply edits locally for instant feedback, then send operations to a collaboration server. The server uses Operational Transformation (OT) or a CRDT to merge concurrent edits deterministically. Transformed operations are broadcast to all other clients. A persistence layer stores the document and its full operation history.

### Key Components
- **Client Editor**: Applies local edits optimistically; queues operations for server sync
- **OT/CRDT Engine**: Transforms concurrent operations to preserve intent (OT) or uses conflict-free merge (CRDT)
- **Collaboration Server**: Serializes operations, assigns global order, broadcasts to clients
- **Document Store**: Persists document snapshots and operation logs for history/undo

### Scaling
Partition by document: each document is handled by one server (sticky sessions). Use sharding across millions of documents. Take periodic snapshots to truncate the operation log.

### Trade-offs
- OT is well-proven (Google Docs) but requires a central server for ordering; CRDTs enable P2P but are more complex and memory-heavy
- Storing every operation enables full revision history but grows storage linearly
- Optimistic local apply provides instant UX but requires complex conflict resolution
10.5.4.intuition: Real-time collaboration is a distributed consensus problem disguised as a text editing feature: every client has a local copy and the system must ensure all copies converge to the same state despite concurrent edits arriving in different orders. OT achieves this by transforming operations against each other; CRDTs achieve it by making operations inherently commutative.

exercises.title: Implement Basic Operational Transformation
exercises.description: Build an OT engine for a plain-text document supporting insert(pos, char) and delete(pos) operations. Write a transform function that correctly handles two concurrent inserts at different positions.
exercises.hint: For two inserts: if op1.pos <= op2.pos, shift op2.pos right by 1 (and vice versa). For insert vs delete: if insert.pos <= delete.pos, shift delete.pos right by 1.

exercises.title: Build a Collaborative Editing Server
exercises.description: Create a WebSocket server that accepts operations from multiple clients, applies OT to resolve conflicts, and broadcasts the transformed operations to all connected clients.
exercises.hint: Maintain a server-side operation log. When a client sends an op based on revision N, transform it against all operations after N in the log before applying and broadcasting.

problems.problem: Documents diverge after a rapid sequence of concurrent edits from 3+ users
problems.cause: The OT transform function does not handle the composition of three-way concurrent transforms correctly
problems.solution: Ensure the server imposes a total order on operations: each client must acknowledge the server's version before sending the next op, and transform against the full server history.

problems.problem: Operation log grows unboundedly for long-lived documents, slowing down new client joins
problems.cause: New clients must replay the entire operation history to reconstruct the document
problems.solution: Periodically snapshot the document state and truncate the log. New clients load the latest snapshot and only replay operations after it.

further_reading.title: Operational Transformation in Real-Time Group Editors
CRDTs: The Hard Parts - Martin Kleppmann

10.6.1.body:
## Design a Distributed Message Queue (Kafka)

### Requirements
- Publish and consume millions of messages per second with low latency
- Guarantee ordering within a partition and at-least-once delivery
- Support consumer groups, offset management, and message replay
- Retain messages for configurable durations (hours to weeks)

### Architecture
Producers send messages to a cluster of brokers. Each topic is split into partitions, distributed across brokers. Messages within a partition are appended to an immutable, sequential log. Consumers in a group are each assigned a subset of partitions and track their offset. Replication across brokers provides fault tolerance.

### Key Components
- **Broker**: Stores partition log segments on disk, serves produce/consume requests
- **Partition Log**: Append-only file with sequential offsets; zero-copy reads via sendfile()
- **Consumer Group Coordinator**: Assigns partitions to consumers, manages rebalancing
- **Replication Manager**: Maintains in-sync replicas (ISR) with leader-follower protocol

### Scaling
Add partitions to increase parallelism; add brokers and rebalance partitions. Producers partition by key hash for ordering guarantees. Use tiered storage to offload old segments to S3.

### Trade-offs
- More partitions increase parallelism but add rebalancing overhead and metadata cost
- At-least-once is default; exactly-once requires idempotent producers and transactional consumers
- Long retention enables replay but increases storage cost; tiered storage mitigates this
10.6.1.intuition: A distributed message queue is an append-only distributed log where the key insight is that sequential disk I/O (appending) is fast enough to rival network throughput, eliminating the need for complex in-memory caching. Partitions are the unit of parallelism, ordering, and fault tolerance all at once.

exercises.title: Implement a Partition Log with Segment Rotation
exercises.description: Build an append-only log that writes to segment files. When a segment exceeds 1MB, rotate to a new segment. Support reading from any offset across segments.
exercises.hint: Maintain an index mapping offset ranges to segment files. On read, binary search the index to find the right segment, then seek to the offset within it.

exercises.title: Build a Consumer Group Rebalancer
exercises.description: Implement partition assignment for a consumer group: when consumers join or leave, reassign partitions evenly. Test with 12 partitions and dynamically adding/removing consumers.
exercises.hint: Use the round-robin or range assignor strategy. On rebalance, sort partitions and consumers, then assign partitions[i] to consumer[i % num_consumers].

problems.problem: Consumer rebalances cause processing pauses of 30+ seconds during deployments
problems.cause: The stop-the-world rebalance protocol revokes all partitions before reassigning, causing a full pause
problems.solution: Use incremental cooperative rebalancing: only revoke partitions that are moving, allowing consumers to continue processing their retained partitions.

problems.problem: A slow consumer causes the entire consumer group to lag behind
problems.cause: One consumer cannot keep up with its assigned partitions, and the group cannot make progress past those offsets
problems.solution: Monitor per-consumer lag and reassign partitions from slow consumers. Consider splitting the slow consumer's partitions to other group members or scaling out.

further_reading.title: The Log: What Every Software Engineer Should Know About Real-Time Data
Kafka: A Distributed Messaging System for Log Processing

10.6.2.body:
## Design an Email Service (Gmail)

### Requirements
- Send and receive emails supporting SMTP, IMAP, and POP3 protocols
- Store billions of emails with full-text search across subject, body, and attachments
- Provide spam filtering, labels/folders, threaded conversations, and attachment handling
- Support push notifications for new mail with sub-second delivery

### Architecture
Incoming mail arrives via SMTP servers, passes through a spam/virus filter pipeline, and is stored in a distributed mail store indexed per user. A search service indexes email content using an inverted index. Outgoing mail goes through a sending pipeline with DKIM/SPF signing. A notification service pushes new-mail events to connected clients.

### Key Components
- **SMTP Gateway**: Receives inbound email, performs MX lookup for outbound routing
- **Spam Filter Pipeline**: Multi-stage filter (SPF/DKIM check, ML classifier, rule engine)
- **Mail Store**: Bigtable-like storage with per-user partitioning and message deduplication
- **Search Index**: Per-user inverted index updated asynchronously on mail receipt

### Scaling
Partition by user ID; each user's mailbox lives on one shard with replication. Blob storage for large attachments (referenced by pointer in the mail store). Separate the search index into its own cluster.

### Trade-offs
- Per-user partitioning simplifies access patterns but creates hot shards for heavy users
- Synchronous spam filtering adds latency to delivery; async risks delivering spam first
- Full-text search of attachments requires OCR/parsing pipelines, adding complexity
10.6.2.intuition: An email service is a per-user document store with a complex ingestion pipeline, where the fundamental design insight is that emails are written once and read many times, so optimizing the read path (search, threading, labeling) matters more than write throughput. Spam filtering is where most of the engineering complexity actually lives.

exercises.title: Build a Threaded Conversation Grouper
exercises.description: Given a set of emails with In-Reply-To and References headers, group them into conversation threads. Handle cases where intermediate emails are missing.
exercises.hint: Build a forest of message IDs using In-Reply-To as parent pointers. For missing intermediates, create placeholder nodes. Merge trees that share References.

exercises.title: Implement a Simple Spam Classifier
exercises.description: Build a Naive Bayes spam classifier trained on a labeled email dataset. Compute precision and recall on a test set. Integrate it as a filter step in a mock email pipeline.
exercises.hint: Tokenize email body, compute P(word|spam) and P(word|ham) from training data. For a new email, multiply probabilities across all words and compare spam vs ham likelihoods.

problems.problem: Search results are missing recently received emails
problems.cause: The search index update is asynchronous and lags behind mail delivery during peak hours
problems.solution: Implement a real-time indexing path for recent emails (last N minutes) that queries the mail store directly, merged with the async index results.

problems.problem: Sending reputation degrades, causing outbound emails to land in recipients' spam folders
problems.cause: Compromised accounts are sending bulk spam through the outbound pipeline, tainting the IP reputation
problems.solution: Implement outbound rate limits per user, detect anomalous sending patterns, and use dedicated IP pools that isolate transactional mail from user-generated mail.

further_reading.title: Gmail Backend Architecture - System Design
Bigtable: A Distributed Storage System for Structured Data

10.6.3.body:
## Design a Logging/Analytics Pipeline

### Requirements
- Ingest terabytes of log data daily from thousands of services and hosts
- Support structured and unstructured log formats with schema evolution
- Provide near real-time search (under 30 seconds from emission to searchable)
- Retain logs for 30-90 days with archival to cold storage for compliance

### Architecture
Log agents on each host tail log files and ship events to a collection tier (Kafka/Fluentd). A stream processing layer enriches, parses, and routes logs by type. Hot logs go to an indexing cluster (Elasticsearch/OpenSearch) for search. Cold logs are archived to S3/GCS in columnar format (Parquet) for batch analytics.

### Key Components
- **Log Agent**: Filebeat/Fluentd on each host; handles backpressure and local buffering
- **Collection Tier**: Kafka topics partitioned by service; decouples producers from consumers
- **Stream Processor**: Flink/Logstash that parses, enriches (adds host/region tags), and routes
- **Index Cluster**: Elasticsearch with time-based indices and ILM (Index Lifecycle Management) for rollover and deletion

### Scaling
Time-based index partitioning (one index per day) for easy retention management. Scale Kafka partitions and ES data nodes independently. Use sampling for very high-volume debug logs.

### Trade-offs
- Indexing everything enables ad-hoc search but is expensive; selective indexing saves cost but requires knowing what to keep upfront
- Shorter index retention reduces cost but limits incident investigation windows
- Structured logging is easier to query but requires upfront schema discipline from developers
10.6.3.intuition: A logging pipeline is a high-throughput ETL system where the key insight is that logs have a natural time dimension that dictates everything: partitioning, retention, and query patterns. The collection tier (Kafka) is the shock absorber that prevents bursty log producers from overwhelming the indexing layer.

exercises.title: Build a Log Pipeline with Backpressure
exercises.description: Create a pipeline: log generator -> in-memory buffer -> batch writer to disk. Implement backpressure so the generator slows down when the buffer is 80% full.
exercises.hint: Use a bounded blocking queue as the buffer. When the queue reaches 80% capacity, have the producer sleep or drop low-priority logs. The consumer batches and writes every N messages or T seconds.

exercises.title: Implement Time-Based Index Rotation
exercises.description: Simulate an indexing system that creates a new index daily, automatically deletes indices older than 7 days, and redirects all writes to the current day's index.
exercises.hint: Use an alias pointing to the current index. A scheduled job creates tomorrow's index, switches the alias, and deletes the index from 8 days ago.

problems.problem: Elasticsearch cluster goes red during log bursts from a single service
problems.cause: One service emits 100x normal log volume during an incident, saturating the index write queue
problems.solution: Implement per-service rate limiting in the stream processor, and use separate Kafka topics or ES indices for high-volume services to isolate impact.

problems.problem: Log searches return incomplete results for recent events
problems.cause: The Elasticsearch refresh interval is set to 30 seconds, so recently indexed logs are not yet searchable
problems.solution: Reduce the refresh interval to 5 seconds for hot indices (accepting higher resource usage), or provide a separate real-time tail view that reads directly from Kafka.

further_reading.title: Building LinkedIn's Real-Time Activity Data Pipeline
Scaling Elasticsearch for Logs at Uber

10.6.4.body:
## Design a Recommendation Engine

### Requirements
- Generate personalized recommendations for millions of users across items (products, content, people)
- Combine collaborative filtering, content-based, and popularity signals
- Serve recommendations with <100ms latency; refresh daily with near real-time boosts
- Handle cold-start for new users and new items

### Architecture
An offline pipeline trains models on user-item interaction data (views, clicks, purchases) using collaborative filtering (matrix factorization, ALS) and content features. Pre-computed recommendations per user are stored in a serving layer. An online layer re-ranks results using real-time signals (session context, recency) before serving.

### Key Components
- **Feature Store**: Stores user profiles, item features, interaction history for training and serving
- **Training Pipeline**: Spark/TensorFlow jobs that train CF and content-based models on interaction matrices
- **Candidate Generation**: Retrieves top-N candidates per user from pre-computed lists or ANN (approximate nearest neighbor) index
- **Ranking Service**: Re-ranks candidates with a lightweight model using real-time features

### Scaling
Pre-compute and cache recommendations per user. Use ANN indexes (FAISS, ScaNN) for real-time candidate retrieval from embedding space. Separate candidate generation (recall) from ranking (precision) to manage compute.

### Trade-offs
- Collaborative filtering is powerful but suffers cold-start; content-based handles new items but lacks serendipity
- Pre-computed recs are fast but stale; real-time models are fresh but expensive
- Optimizing for engagement may create filter bubbles; diversity injection hurts click-through but improves satisfaction
10.6.4.intuition: A recommendation engine is a two-stage system: a broad candidate generation phase that retrieves hundreds of plausible items, followed by a precise ranking phase that scores and orders them using richer features. The cold-start problem is solved not by one technique but by gracefully falling back through a hierarchy of signals: collaborative, content-based, then popularity.

exercises.title: Implement Matrix Factorization with ALS
exercises.description: Build a basic Alternating Least Squares recommender on the MovieLens 100K dataset. Split into train/test, compute RMSE, and generate top-10 recommendations for a user.
exercises.hint: Initialize user and item factor matrices randomly. Alternate: fix items, solve for users (least squares); fix users, solve for items. Repeat for 10-20 iterations.

exercises.title: Build a Two-Stage Recommend-and-Rank Pipeline
exercises.description: Implement candidate generation using item-item similarity (cosine on feature vectors) and a ranking stage that re-orders candidates by a weighted score combining similarity, recency, and popularity.
exercises.hint: For candidate generation, pre-compute item-item similarity matrix and retrieve top-50 similar items to user's recent interactions. For ranking, combine scores as: alpha*similarity + beta*recency + gamma*popularity.

problems.problem: Recommendations become stale and repetitive for active users
problems.cause: The offline model only retrains daily, so today's interactions are not reflected in recommendations
problems.solution: Add a real-time boosting layer that promotes items similar to the user's current session activity, and penalize already-seen items.

problems.problem: New items never get recommended because they lack interaction data
problems.cause: Collaborative filtering cannot score items with no user interactions (cold-start problem)
problems.solution: Use content-based features (title, category, description embeddings) to place new items in the embedding space, and inject them into candidate generation with an exploration budget.

System Design: Recommendation System

10.6.5.body:
## Design a CI/CD Pipeline (GitHub Actions)

### Requirements
- Trigger builds on push, pull request, schedule, or manual dispatch
- Execute multi-step workflows with parallel and sequential jobs on isolated runners
- Support artifact caching, secret management, and environment-specific deployments
- Scale to thousands of concurrent builds with job queuing and prioritization

### Architecture
A webhook listener receives events from the source control system and enqueues workflow runs. A scheduler matches jobs to available runners from a pool of ephemeral VMs/containers. Each runner executes steps in an isolated environment, streaming logs back to the control plane. An artifact store holds build outputs and caches.

### Key Components
- **Webhook/Event Listener**: Receives SCM events, evaluates trigger conditions, creates workflow runs
- **Scheduler**: Matches pending jobs to runners by label (OS, architecture), manages queue priority
- **Runner Pool**: Ephemeral VMs or containers provisioned on demand; cleaned up after each job
- **Artifact & Cache Store**: S3-backed storage for build artifacts, dependency caches, and logs

### Scaling
Auto-scale runner pools based on queue depth. Use spot/preemptible instances for cost savings with checkpointing for interrupted jobs. Cache dependency downloads aggressively to reduce build times.

### Trade-offs
- Ephemeral runners are clean and secure but have cold-start overhead; persistent runners are faster but risk contamination
- Shared caches speed up builds but introduce cache invalidation complexity
- Self-hosted runners give control but require maintenance; managed runners are convenient but limit customization
10.6.5.intuition: A CI/CD pipeline is a distributed job execution engine triggered by source control events, where the central challenge is balancing the throughput of thousands of concurrent builds against the isolation guarantees each build needs. Ephemeral runners provide the cleanest isolation but make caching the critical optimization to avoid rebuilding the world every time.

exercises.title: Build a DAG-Based Workflow Executor
exercises.description: Implement a workflow engine that parses a YAML-defined job DAG (with 'needs' dependencies), runs independent jobs in parallel, and respects sequential dependencies. Track pass/fail status.
exercises.hint: Topologically sort the job DAG. Use a thread pool: enqueue all jobs with zero unmet dependencies. When a job completes, decrement dependency counts of its children and enqueue newly-unblocked jobs.

exercises.title: Implement Dependency Caching for Builds
exercises.description: Create a cache layer keyed by a hash of lock files (e.g., package-lock.json). On cache hit, restore the dependency directory; on miss, run install and upload the result.
exercises.hint: Compute SHA-256 of the lock file as cache key. Check the artifact store for that key. On hit, download and extract. On miss, run install, tar the result, and upload with the key.

problems.problem: Builds queue for 10+ minutes during peak hours despite available cloud capacity
problems.cause: The runner auto-scaler reacts too slowly, waiting for sustained queue depth before provisioning new VMs
problems.solution: Implement predictive scaling based on historical patterns (e.g., post-standup push spikes), and reduce the scale-up cooldown period. Pre-warm a baseline pool for expected peak.

problems.problem: A secret is exposed in build logs, creating a security incident
problems.cause: A build step accidentally prints an environment variable containing a secret to stdout
problems.solution: Implement automatic secret masking in the log streaming layer: scan output for known secret values and replace them with '***'. Also audit and rotate the exposed secret.

further_reading.title: How GitHub Actions Works Behind the Scenes
Scaling CI/CD at Shopify

10.6.6.body:
## Design a Multi-Tenant SaaS Platform

### Requirements
- Serve thousands of tenants (organizations) from a shared infrastructure
- Isolate tenant data, enforce per-tenant resource quotas, and prevent noisy-neighbor effects
- Support tenant-specific customization (branding, feature flags, integrations)
- Provide tenant onboarding, billing metering, and admin dashboards

### Architecture
A shared application tier routes requests by tenant ID (extracted from subdomain or JWT). A data tier supports multiple isolation models: shared database with tenant_id column, schema-per-tenant, or database-per-tenant for premium tiers. A control plane manages tenant configuration, feature flags, and quota enforcement.

### Key Components
- **Tenant Router**: Extracts tenant context from request, applies rate limits, routes to appropriate resources
- **Data Isolation Layer**: Row-level security (RLS) in shared DB or separate schemas/databases per tenant
- **Quota/Rate Limiter**: Per-tenant rate limiting and resource quotas (API calls, storage, compute)
- **Billing Meter**: Tracks usage events per tenant for usage-based billing; emits to billing system

### Scaling
Use shared infrastructure for small tenants (cost-efficient) and dedicated resources for large/enterprise tenants (performance isolation). Shard the shared database by tenant_id. Cache tenant configs aggressively.

### Trade-offs
- Shared DB is cost-efficient but risks data leaks via bugs; separate DBs are safer but expensive
- Uniform infrastructure simplifies ops but cannot meet diverse tenant SLAs; tiered offerings add complexity
- Tenant customization increases flexibility but multiplies testing surface area
10.6.6.intuition: Multi-tenancy is the art of sharing infrastructure across customers while making each customer feel like they have their own dedicated system. The core architectural decision is the data isolation model, which exists on a spectrum from fully shared (cheap, risky) to fully isolated (expensive, safe), and most real systems offer tiered options.

exercises.title: Implement Row-Level Tenant Isolation
exercises.description: Create a shared PostgreSQL database with a tenant_id column on all tables. Implement row-level security policies so that queries from tenant A can never see tenant B's data, even with SQL injection.
exercises.hint: Use PostgreSQL RLS: CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON table USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant')). Set the session variable on each request before executing queries.

exercises.title: Build a Per-Tenant Rate Limiter
exercises.description: Implement a token bucket rate limiter that enforces different rate limits per tenant (e.g., free: 100 req/min, pro: 1000 req/min). Test with concurrent requests from multiple tenants.
exercises.hint: Store a token bucket per tenant (tokens, last_refill_time) in Redis. On each request, refill based on elapsed time, then decrement. Reject if tokens <= 0. Load the rate limit from tenant config.

problems.problem: A large tenant's heavy queries slow down the shared database for all other tenants
problems.cause: All tenants share the same database connection pool and the large tenant's queries consume most connections and I/O
problems.solution: Implement per-tenant connection pool limits, add query timeouts, and migrate large tenants to dedicated database instances with a seamless routing change.

problems.problem: A developer accidentally deploys a migration that exposes tenant A's data to tenant B
problems.cause: A new query was added without a WHERE tenant_id clause, bypassing the application-level isolation
problems.solution: Enforce row-level security at the database level (not just application level) as a safety net, and add automated tests that verify cross-tenant data isolation on every migration.

further_reading.title: Multi-Tenant Architecture for SaaS - AWS Well-Architected
Building Multi-Tenant SaaS with PostgreSQL Row-Level Security
